For year 4:
Alternatively, a working could be presented utilising a data entry area for the inflation
rates (expressed as a decimal):
For year 2:
And so on for year 3 and 4.
Finally, the spreadsheet functionality can also be used to efficiently compute the
project’s NPV*:
Here, for the financial acceptability comment it is sufficient for one mark to state
“NPV is positive, therefore accept the investment”.
*use of the spreadsheet formulae yield slight rounding differences compared to the
suggested solution, both here and in subsequent illustrations.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2023 13
Requirement (b)(ii) – 3 marks
(b)(ii) Calculate the sensitivity of the project's net present value to a change
in the level of contribution per unit; and
This requirement should be approached by making use of the sensitivity formula
which, when asked for project sensitivity to a change in the level of contribution per
unit, is:
Sensitivity %  =  NPV / Present value of post-tax contribution
Therefore, candidates firstly need to isolate the present value (PV) of post-tax
contribution, as per the suggested solution, remembering to apply the tax effect one
year in arrears as per the case.
The PV of the post-tax contribution can be found efficiently by using the NPV
formula:
Therefore,
Sensitivity %  =  (5,858 / 51,269*) x 100%
= 11.4%
As has been reported in previous Examiner’s Reports, when asked to perform a
sensitivity analysis calculation, the basic structure of the percentage computation is
usually reproduced and performed by prepared candidates, but too many candidates
fail to take into account the tax effects of a change in a variable such as contribution
per unit, or, as noted above, the payment of tax one year in arrears. Moreover, some
candidates ignore the need to express the denominator of the sensitivity computation
in present value terms.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2023 14
Requirement (b)(iii) – 3 marks
(b)(iii) Calculate the sensitivity of the project's net present value to a change
in the discount rate.
This requirement should be approached by firstly calculating the internal rate of
return (IRR) of the project, and then measuring the sensitivity of the change in the
rate from the original discount rate.
The project IRR can be computed efficiently by using the IRR spreadsheet function:
The IRR of 18.4%* is the discount rate at which the project’s NPV = 0, therefore at
an after-tax discount rate above 18.4% the project is not acceptable.
Therefore, the sensitivity of the project's net present value to a change in the
discount rate can computed by comparing the change from the original discount rate
to the original discount rate itself:
Sensitivity % = ((18.4 – 11) / 11) x 100%
= (7.4 / 11) x 100%
=  67.3%
When asked to perform sensitivity analysis on the discount rate, computation of the
IRR was usually well executed by candidates but there were issues when using the
IRR to calculate discount rate sensitivity. The difference between the calculated IRR
figure and the company’s discount rate was sometimes shown incorrectly as the
discount rate sensitivity itself. Also, there were occasions where the incorrect ‘base’
was chosen from which to calculate the percentage change. Percentage changes
are always computed using the original figure as the base.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2023 15
Requirement (c) – 6 marks
(c) Compare and contrast sensitivity analysis and probability analysis as
methods of assisting investment decisions when there is more than one
possible outcome.
Syllabus area D also requires candidates to be able to discuss the usefulness of
sensitivity analysis and probability analysis in assisting investment decisions.
A good approach to this type of question is to analyse the question requirement by
firstly identifying the verb – the verbs used in the requirements not only indicate what
to do but often how much detail to go into. Here the requirement is asking candidates
to “compare and contrast” for 6 marks.
This requires consideration of each point for both methods eg likelihood of
outcomes, where the contrast is between probability analysis, showing how likely it is
that outcomes will occur that will change the decision, and sensitivity analysis, where
the probabilities of the outcomes occurring are unknown. A similar contrast can be
made for decision-making and the number of variables, as per the suggested
solution.
Unfortunately, candidates’ responses were often too short, expressing little more
than a definition of each type of analysis, to have any chance of getting the marks on
offer. Furthermore, responses were too often of variable quality and a number of
answers were often disorganised and poorly expressed such as providing a list of
brief points, evidencing a lack of planning or maybe a lack of knowledge.
Few candidates could clearly compare and contrast the two methods. Instead, with
respect to each method, many appeared to write all that they knew about sensitivity
analysis and then all that they knew about probability analysis. Whilst (very) basic
definitions were seen frequently, deeper knowledge, such as comparatively
explaining the usefulness of each method for decision making, was rare.
Previously published questions containing some aspects of the areas examined in
this question include Melplash Co (March / June 2022) Crocket Co (September /
December 2020), and Vyxyn Co (March / June 2017).
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2023 16
Question 2 – Marble Co
Marble is from the Business Finance (E) area of the syllabus, specifically:
Part (a) tests E2(a), “Estimate the cost of equity”, E2(b) “Estimating the cost of debt”
and E2(c) “Estimating the overall cost of capital”.  First, candidates are expected to
apply the dividend growth model to enable the calculation of the cost of equity.
Second, the cost of debt, which includes preference shares and convertible loan
notes, needs to be calculated. Finally, the weighted average cost of capital (WACC)
had to be calculated using market values.
Part (b) tests E2(a)(i), “application of the dividend growth model, its assumptions,
advantages and disadvantages” and E2(a)(iv), “application of the CAPM, its
assumptions, advantages and disadvantages”.
Part (c) tests E3(b), “Describe the creditor hierarchy and its connection with the
relative costs of sources of finance”.
Marble Co is similar to previous questions examined in this area. The cost of debt
calculation includes irredeemable, redeemable, and bank debt. Candidates must
familiarise themselves with all these finance sources, estimate the cost of debt and
finally incorporate each component correctly into the WACC calculation. Additionally,
the cost of equity can be derived using the capital asset pricing model (CAPM) but
this was not possible in Marble Co as there was no information relevant to using
CAPM.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2023 17
Requirement (a) - 11 marks
(a) Using market values, calculate the after-tax weighted average cost of
capital of Marble Co.
Most candidates scored high marks on this question and performed well on the
numerical part. Candidates could calculate the dividend growth rate from the
information provided in the scenario and use this to calculate the cost of equity.
Although different methods are available to calculate the dividend growth rate, the
easiest way to calculate the dividend growth rate is to use the information provided
to find the growth rates for the dividend per share.
Step 1 – Calculate the cost of equity
(i) First, find the growth rate (g) over a four-year period since dividends per
share have grown from $0.69 in 20X4 to $0.84 in 20X8 and, therefore:
(ii) Input all the figures in the dividend growth model formula to calculate the
cost of equity (Ke). Two problems noted here were: candidates did not use
the ex-dividend share price of $11.09, giving Ke 13% and candidates often
ignored g.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2023 18
Step 2 – Calculate the cost of preference shares
Candidates should recognise preference shares are irredeemable, preference
dividends are not tax deductible and dividends paid are constant. Hence, the cost of
preference shares (Kp) is simply the dividend per share payable divided by the ex-
dividend market price of the preference share.
Step 3 – Calculate the cost of convertible debt after tax (Kdat)
Some candidates found this problematic. To fully score all the available marks,
candidates need to do the following: (i) calculate the conversion value for the loan
notes, (ii) provide a justified comparison between the redemption value ($100) and
the conversion value ($118.73) to decide whether conversion is likely to happen (iii)
calculate the after-tax annual interest payment ($4.81) and finally, ensure that you
know which values to use for the market value in year 0 and the redemption value in
year 5. You are now ready to calculate the net present values and the internal rate of
return, which gives Kdat. Please see the answer for the setting up of the linear
interpolation. Alternatively, you can calculate the internal rate of return using the
Excel formula. You must correctly recognise inflows and outflows.
For the reasons noted above, candidates made several errors in calculating Kdat.
Step 4 – Calculate the market values of the different sources of finance: ordinary
shares, preference shares and convertible loan notes.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2023 19
This caused little problems for most of the candidates. However, some candidates
used book values instead of market values. Given that the scenario requirement is to
calculate WACC using market values., therefore, calculations based on book values
scored no marks.
Step 5 – Calculate the WACC
Marble Co has three components to the WACC calculation:
Please note that to display the formula in Excel, the = at the beginning of each
formula has been omitted. Use the spreadsheet when laying out your answer and
show all workings with the cells, referring to other cells where possible, as this helps
avoid potential mistyping of numbers.
Requirement (b) - 4 marks
(b) Discuss the relative merits of the capital asset pricing model and the
dividend growth model in calculating the cost of equity
The requirement expected candidates to discuss the “relative merits” of the capital
asset pricing model (CAPM) and the dividend growth model (DGM) as required in
Section E of the Financial Management syllabus (2a i and iv, p.17). The comparison
of the CAPM and DGM is discussed in an article on the ACCA website, in study
texts, and illustrated by the suggested solution. Whilst there were some good
responses, many answers showed limited understanding of this syllabus area. Too
often, responses were irrelevant to the requirement. e.g. how to calculate a project-
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2023 20
specific discount rate or written in a general way about CAPM with no attempt to
discuss the relative merit of CAPM over DGM or vice versa, often taking instead
individual aspects of the CAPM/DGM.
Additionally, candidates need to be able to apply and analyse both CAPM and DGM.
Question requirements need to be addressed directly rather than an approach
whereby candidates write all they know about a topic with the incorrect expectation
that it will score sufficient marks. Since these two models are frequently examined in
the financial management exam, it was surprising that many candidates in part b did
not respond to the requirement. One approach would be to discuss the risk, both
implied and explicit, for both CAPM and DGM, followed by the advantages and or
weaknesses of both models. Awarding more than 1 or 2 marks for this part of the
question was challenging.
Requirement (c) - 5 marks
(c) Explain the connection between the creditor hierarchy and the relative
costs of sources of finance.
The requirement expected candidates to explain the creditor hierarchy and the
relative costs of sources of finance as required in Section E (3b, p.17) of the
Financial Management syllabus. A good approach to answering this question is to
begin by explaining the creditor hierarchy. Candidates were aware of the different
sources of finance; however, they scored low marks as the creditor hierarchy was
shown as a list and then lacked significant relevant knowledge to show adequate
discussion of the relationship between the creditor hierarchy and the relative costs.
Giving a list without any discussion does not score any marks. Awarding more than 1
or 2 marks for this part of the question was often challenging. Some candidates did
not gain marks in part (c) by discussing concepts often at length that did not address
the requirement, such as capital rationing.
Practice the published questions and other questions on the Practice Platform and
also in the materials from ACCA’s Approved Content Providers and identify the
learning outcomes as prescribed in the FM syllabus. This will help you with the
relevant topics and what the exam will test for both the numerical and discussion
questions.

1. The information provided needs to be read carefully and understood. It is worth
remembering that the information is provided for a purpose, to assist in the answering of
the question.
Examiner’s report – FM - March/June 2024  11

2. Breaking down the requirement - there are three ratios to calculate under  both the loan
note issue, part (a)(i), and three ratios under the rights issue of equity, part (a)(ii).

3. Presentation - calculations are required so consideration must be given to how they are
presented. Both options will mean that new figures will need to be in the income statement.
A suggestion as to how this should be presented appears below. It is important that all
workings are shown.

4. Verbs used in the requirement for (a)(iii) – ‘briefly comment’ and ‘recommend’. Based upon
the calculated ratios, suitable comments and a justified recommendation can be made.
The full solution to the question can be found on the ACCA Practice Platform
, and it shows
the correct approach to the calculations which should be studied. What follows is further
illustration and advice to be followed for candidates preparing to sit examinations.
Requirement (a)(i) – 4 marks
(a) (i) Calculate the above three ratios for next year, assuming the project is financed
by the loan note issue (option 1).
(4 marks)
Gamgee is proposing an expansion plan involving an investment of $360m, which will earn an
operating profit of $64.8m. This is in addition to current operations, and not in place of them.
Such an expansion will increase the operating profit and will increase the finance costs when
loan notes are used. It is advised that candidates set out the changes to the income statement
in conventional form e.g.
Examiner’s report – FM - March/June 2024  12
Here, we can clearly see the effect of both the additional profit of $64.8m and the cost of the
additional finance of $18m (5% x $360m) . Efficient use of the spreadsheet cells will enable
the recalculation of profit before tax, taxation at 20% and profit after tax (PAT).
(It is appreciated that the calculation of PAT could be executed in one cell only, but laying out
the workings in the organised manner shown above is beneficial)
Interest cover
Now, we are in a good position to calculate interest cover:
$241.8m / $45m = 5.4 times.
EPS
Next, we can approach the calculation of EPS, noting that the number of shares is unchanged
when the project is financed under the loan note issue:
EPS = PAT / Number of shares = $157.4m / 800m = $0.19675
(rounded to $0.20 in the suggested solution).
Some candidates did not calculate PAT, and therefore could not score the marks for EPS. The
calculation of EPS using operating profit is technically incorrect.
Total gearing
Finally, the gearing ratio can be calculated using the formula given in both the case and the
requirement i.e. debt / (debt plus equity) using market values.
The debt increases from the original value of $450m due to the loan note issue raising $360m:
$450m + $360m = $810m
The equity needs to be valued at market value.
The number of shares in issue is unchanged at 800 million.
Examiner’s report – FM - March/June 2024  13
But the value of each share will change as a result of the new project and this is where careful
study of the information in the case is needed, as it is stated that the P/E ratio is 8 and will
remain unchanged.
Therefore, the new market value per share can be calculated:
P/E ratio x EPS = 8 x $0.20 = $1.60
Hence, with 800m shares in issue, the new market value of equity is:
Market value per share x number of shares = $1.60 x 800m = $1,280m
(Alternatively, the market value of equity can be calculated by using the PAT:
$157.4m x 8 = $1,262.4m.
There is a difference due to the EPS having been rounded up to $0.20)
Some candidates did not realise that the EPS figure was required in the calculation of the
market value of equity, and incorrectly assumed that the market price per share would be
unchanged at $1.20 ($0.15 x 8).
Now the gearing ratio can be correctly calculated using the debt and equity values above:
D / (D+E) = $810m / ($810m + $1,280m) = $810m / $2,090m = 38.8%
Requirement (a)(ii) – 4 marks
(a) (ii)
Calculate the above three ratios for next year, assuming the project is financed
by the rights issue of equity (option 2).
(4 marks)
This requirement should be approached in the same manner as (a)(i), but the key difference
is that the new project is financed via a right issue of equity and not via debt.
The extract from the income statement can be recalculated:
Examiner’s report – FM - March/June 2024  14
Interest cover
Now, we can recalculate interest cover:
$241.8m / $27m = 9.0 times (rounded).
EPS
As in (a)(i), then we can approach the calculation of earnings per share (EPS), noting that the
number of shares changes as the project is now financed by a rights issue of equity.
The case states that under option 2, a rights issue of equity will be at a 25% discount to the
current share price, which can be shown as 75% of the current share price:
75% x $1.20 = $0.90 per share
With $360m of new finance needed, the number of new shares can be calculated:
$360m / $0.90 per share = 400m shares.
Therefore, the total number of shares after the rights issue:
800m issued shares + 400m new shares = 1,200m shares.
EPS can now be calculated:
EPS = PAT / Number of shares = $171.8m / 1,200m = $0.14 (rounded)
As in (a)(i), some candidates did not calculate PAT, and therefore could not score the marks
for EPS. To restate, the calculation of EPS using operating profit is technically incorrect.
Total gearing
Finally, the gearing ratio can be calculated using the formula given in both the case and the
requirement i.e. debt / (debt plus equity) using market values.
The debt is unchanged at the original value of $450m as given in the case.
The equity needs to be valued at market value.
The number of shares in issue is now 1,200 million.
As in (a)(i), the value of each share will change as a result of the new project and, once again,
this is where the stated P/E ratio of 8 is needed.
Therefore, the new market value per share can be calculated:
P/E ratio x EPS = 8 x $0.14 = $1.12
Hence, with 1,200m shares in issue, the new market value of equity is:
Examiner’s report – FM - March/June 2024  15
Market value per share x number of shares = $1.12 x 1,200m = $1,344m
(Alternatively, the market value of equity can be calculated by using the PAT:
$171.8m x 8 = $1,374.4m.
There is a difference due to the EPS having been rounded down to $0.14)
As in (a)(i), some candidates did not realise that the EPS figure was required in the calculation
of the market value of equity.
Now the gearing ratio can be correctly calculated using the debt and equity values above:
D / (D+E) = $450m / ($450m + $1,344m) = $450m / $1,794m = 25.1%
In both (a)(i) and (a)(ii), gearing ratios using debt / equity or using book values were not
creditworthy.
For parts such as these, the layout of workings is of high importance. For some candidates,
this still needs improvement and too many candidates showed no workings at all, and just
entered incorrect figures into a cell, with no cell formula to support the figure(s), thereby
making it extremely difficult to award marks for workings.
Another question covering this topic in a similar way was Graffham Co (March/June 2023).
The technical article ‘Analysing the suitability of financing alternatives’
, available as part of
Financial Management’s Technical Articles, is also of use to future candidates.
Requirement (a)(iii) – 3 marks
(a) (iii) Using the calculations from (i) and (ii), briefly comment on the results and
recommend which source of finance Gamgee Co’s board of directors should choose.
The requirement here is  to comment on the results from part (a)(i) and (a)(ii) and to
recommend which source of finance should be used.
Better responses will contrast the increase in financial risk under the loan note issue due to
lower interest cover and higher gearing with the increase in shareholder wealth shown by the
rise in EPS. A comparison could then be made with the same financial indicators when the
project is financed by the use of a rights issue of equity.
A justified recommendation could then be made based upon the comments, stating clearly
which source of finance should be chosen.
Whilst there were some good comments and recommendations, too many candidates simply
did not do enough for the 3 marks on offer.
In (a)(iii), many comments were simply mentioning that each ratio had gone up or down without
stating if it was good or bad for the company, thereby failing to demonstrate FM knowledge.
Examiner’s report – FM - March/June 2024  16
Too many gave a one line answer stating that based on the ratios they should pick loan notes
or rights issue without any justification or further explanation. Several responses  incorrectly
recommended both options when a choice was recommended.
Requirement (b) – 5 marks
(b) Discuss the problems associated with high levels of financial gearing.
This requirement was the best answered part of the question, with m any candidates being
able to identify more than one problem associated with high levels of financial gearing.
Problems which could lead to marks being scored are set out in the suggested solution,
including bankruptcy risk, agency costs/restrictive conditions, debt capacity, increase in
financing costs and tax exhaustion.
Marks were awarded on the basis of one mark per valid point. Given the wording of the
requirement (‘problems’), it was expected that more than one problem would be discussed.
As per the suggested solution, any problem identified needed to be discussed in order to gain
marks. For example, if ‘increase in financing costs’ was identified as a problem, a discussion
similar to ‘shareholders will view dividend pay -out in a highly geared company as risky as
profits could be consumed by high levels of interest’ would be regarded as a valid point. If
developed into a discussion of how or why this is important such as ‘under the risk return
trade-off this will mean that shareholders in a highly geared company will require a higher rate
of return, increasing the cost of equity’ then a further valid point is creditworthy.
The above example illustrates the need for the required discussion to demonstrate relevant
FM knowledge.
Weaker responses did not develop points in the manner suggested above or gave very brief
answers, such as a list of short points when a discussion was required. Some answers did not
directly address the requirement, for example just covering theories of capital structure and
therefore could only gain marks on the high gearing aspects.
The problems associated with high levels of gearing also featured in the question Zeddermore
Co (March/June 2021).
Requirement (c) – 4 marks
(c) Discuss TWO sources of Islamic finance that could satisfy Gamgee Co’s needs for
the project, taking into account the board’s condition.
This part-question required candidates to identify sources of Islamic finance (IF), in particular
to select two sources relevant to the circumstances of Gamgee Co.
The ACCA syllabus lists five different IF instruments available to businesses: Murabaha (trade
credit), Ijara (lease finance), Mudaraba (equity finance), Sukuk (debt finance) and Musharaka
(venture capital).
Examiner’s report – FM - March/June 2024  17
The case states the board’s condition that ‘any Islamic finance arrangement should be similar
to traditional debt with no profit sharing or management involvement from the investor/Islamic
bank.’ Therefore, selecting sources which did not fulfil the criteria would score no marks. As
the note under the suggested solution states:
“Mudaraba and Musharaka types would not be suitable as they are similar to traditional equity
with profit sharing (both) and IF Institution management involvement under the Musharaka.”
This leaves Murabaha, Ijara and Sukuk as relevant sources given the board’s condition.
Given that there are four marks available for two sources of IF, then it is clear that a discussion
sufficient for two marks is required for each selected source.
As per the suggested solution, each relevant source needed to be described correctly for one
mark and then discussed in context, e.g. why the source has been selected given the board’s
condition, for a second mark.
For example, where Murabaha was chosen a description of the nature of the finance, ‘Under
a Murabaha, the Islamic Finance Institution (IFI) would purchase the assets for the project and
then sell them to Gamgee Co who would pay the IFI in fixed instalments’ could be
supplemented by points about its similarity to traditional debt, ‘a 10-ye ar term (same as the
proposed loan notes) should be possible’ and lack of management involvement. ‘the IFI would
not have any involvement in the running of Gamgee Co.’
As with part (b) above, too many candidates  did not develop points in the manner suggested
above or gave very brief answers, such as simply stating two types of IF, often just two words,
when a discussion was required.
Furthermore, some answers discussed IF in a more general manner such as a discussion
about the eligibility of the business activities (the case states that the Gamgee’s proposed
activities are eligible) and/or discussion about the prohibition of the payment of interest.
Neither of these points address the requirement.
The question Graffham Co (March/June 2023) featured IF, but note that Graffham required a
discussion about Mudaraba and Musharaka, the two types of IF not required in Gamgee’s
circumstances. Nonetheless, the two questions taken together with the technical article
‘Introduction to Islamic Finance’, available as part of Financial Management’s
Technical
Articles, ar e all of use to future candidates.
Elaan Co
This question is from section D of the syllabus, Investment Appraisal.
Part (a) addresses syllabus item D1I ; calculate net present value and discuss its usefulness
as an investment appraisal method.
Examiner’s report – FM - March/June 2024  18
Part (b) addresses syllabus item D2(a);  apply and discuss the real -terms and nominal-terms
approaches to investment appraisal.
Part (c) address syllabus item D3(d); Apply and discuss other techniques of adjusting for risk
and uncertainty in investment appraisal, including: (i) simulation, (ii) adjusted payback, (iii)
risk-adjusted discount rates.
Elaan Co is currently reviewing an investment project to acquire and run a hotel. The plan is
for the hotel to provide standard accommodation and a restaurant. The intention is to operate
the hotel for four years and then sell it at the end of year four. We are provided with information
and details about the investment in order to calculate the project’s net present value. All of the
information should be studied carefully before attempting the requirements.
The full solution to the question can be found on the ACCA Practice Platform
, and it shows
the correct approach to the calculations which should be studied. What follows is further
illustration and advice to be followed for candidates preparing to sit examinations.
Requirement (a) – 12 marks
(a) Using a nominal-terms approach, calculate the net present value (NPV) of the project
and comment on its financial acceptability to Elaan Co.
This should be a straightforward requirement with all the information clearly provided in the
scenario. There are a few issues to consider before tackling this part:

1. The duration of the investment project—this requirement is very clearly stated: “The hotel
will be operated for a four -year period and then sold at the end of year four.” So, your
NPV presentation should include years 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4.

2. Presentation—Calculations are required for this requirement, so consideration must be
given to how they are presented. Calculations must support the decision of whether to
accept or reject based on the calculated NPV. In addition, the requirement expected
candidates to comment on the financial acceptability, so this needs to be clearly shown.

3. Breaking down the requirement, it is advisable that a separate working should be shown
where it may be complex to write a simple formula in Excel to avoid errors.
Revenue
Many candidates did not calculate total room nights for 365 days, ignoring the percentage of
occupancy, 365 days or both, and this affected their answers.
“The daily room rate will be $85 for the first year and then increase by 6% per year afterwards”
Therefore, the room rate of $85 should be in year 1 and then inflated for years 2, 3 and 4. It
was important to get the calculation of accommodation right as the following figures are
derived from it: restaurant revenue, variable cost, tax liability and incremental working capital.
Examiner’s report – FM - March/June 2024  19
Some candidates did not include restaurant revenue as part of the total income. One needs
to develop skills in ensuring all items are addressed in the answer. This comes with practicing
exam-style questions.
It is easier to work in thousands, as this will avoid input errors when using smaller numbers.
Some students did use $ million but were not consistent on all numbers and, therefore, made
errors and lost marks.
Additionally, candidates can make use of the round function in the spreadsheet e.g.
=round(cell,0)
Costs
Variable costs depended on the total revenue of accommodation and restaurant revenue. This
caused few problems.
Fixed costs: “Incremental fixed costs are expected to be $433,000 for the first year and then
increase by 4% per year afterwards”, Just like the room rate year 1 figure, fixed costs should
be $433,000 in Year 1.
It is important to show the costs as a negative as this will avoid adding costs to taxable cash
flow.
Tax liability
Some candidates included working capital and/or the residual value of $7,600 in the taxable
cash flow and therefore lost a mark for the tax liability.
TAD benefits
It was pleasing that many candidates calculated TAD benefits correctly, including the timing
and the balancing allowance in year 4.  However, there are still problems with dealing with
residual value and hence the balancing allowance in year 4. to the inclusion of an additional
sale value of $100,000 in year 4 for fixtures.
The calculation of the correct TAD benefits in year 4 is shown below:
Balancing allowance ($000s):  1500 – 375 – 281 – 211 - 100 = 533
TAD benefit year 4 ($000s): 533 @ 20% = 106.6
Incremental working capital
This calculation depended on the total of accommodation revenue and the restaurant revenue.
Candidates easily calculated 5% of total revenue, but then the incremental working capital had
problems. These included the following: timing, incorrect signs, and using an incorrect
percentage.
Additionally, candidates did not click on the correct cells, contributing to more errors and not
getting the full marks available for incremental working capital.
Nominal after-tax cost of capital
Examiner’s report – FM - March/June 2024  20
Questions vary here, and you may be given the nominal cost of capital or the real cost of
capital or sufficient information to calculate the appropriate cost of capital
In this question, the real after -tax cost of capital is 4.8%, and the general rate of inflation is

5.0% per year; hence, using the Fisher formula, the nominal cost of capital is 10.0%:
((1 + 0.048) * (1 + 0.05)) - 1 = (1.1004) – 1 = 10%
Time management
It is important that candidates do not spend more time than allocated to part (a). This is
possibly due to the amount of work required in calculating revenue, TAD benefits, incremental
working capital, and the nominal cost of capital, as well as clear presentation using Excel with
narratives. This was a complicated NPV where students could go wrong in many places.
Requirement (b) – 4 marks
(b) Explain how the approach to the NPV appraisal in part (a) would change if a real-
terms approach had been used.
Candidates easily scored 2 out of 4 marks here, mainly for recognising the difference between
general and specific inflation, how to convert nominal cash flows to real cash flows, and how
to use the real rate of cost of capital to discount the cash flows.
The requirement expected candidates to explain how the NPV in an investment appraisal
changes if a real-term approach is used instead of a nominal approach. This is an important
topic covered in the Financial Management paper, and it is crucial to have a solid grasp of the
distinctions between nominal terms and real terms approaches to investment appraisal.
Syllabus item D2(a) covers the nominal terms and real terms approaches using the net present
value method and necessitates an appreciation of the implications of taxation within this
framework. Candidates who are not adequately prepared often struggle with this part of the
syllabus.
This type of question can be numerical, a discussion, or both. However, this question
specifically stated that no calculations are required. Candidates are required to understand
the requirements of an exam question, so any calculations would not have scored any marks,
and valuable time would have been lost.
Candidates need to clearly explain the following to score full marks:
• Difference between general and specific inflation
• Changes will be reflected in the cost of capital and the cash flows
• Effect of taxation
Cost of capital
A relationship between the nominal and the real cost of capital must be established. The cost
of capital is  normally given in nominal terms (this was calculated in part (a)). Hence, the
Examiner’s report – FM - March/June 2024  21
discussion may state that using the information from part (a), the real cost of capital is
calculated by deflating the nominal cost of capital with the general rate of inflation. However,
in this example, the real cost of capital was given (4.8%).
Cashflows
Provide an explanation of nominal cash flows i.e., current price inflated into future values with
either general or specific inflation (applied in part (a)).
• Most candidates recognised that real cash flows are calculated by deflating nominal cash
flows.
• Most candidates recognised that using a real-terms approach in an investment appraisal
requires using the real cost of capital in calculating the NPV.
Taxation
• The annual tax liability will now need to be revised based on the real cashflows before
tax.
• However, there is no change to the TAD benefits.
Finally, real cash flows are discounted using the real after-tax cost of capital, which for Elaan’s
project is 4.8%.
Please see the technical article on inflation investment appraisal
.
Requirement (c) – 4 marks
(c) Explain how simulation analysis could be used by Elaan Co in its appraisal of the
hotel project AND the expected benefits of using simulation analysis.
It was very rare that all the steps of the simulation process were explained. Candidates were
also expected to explain the expected benefits of using simulation analysis for Elaan Co.
Many candidates seemed to lack the knowledge required to make a substantive response to
this requirement.  When preparing for the exam, it is important to bear in mind that all syllabus
areas can be tested; therefore, a significant knowledge of the syllabus is required.
Practice the published questions and other questions on the Practice Platform and also in
the materials from ACCA’s Approved Content Providers and identify the learning outcomes
as prescribed in the FM syllabus. This will help you with the relevant topics and what the
exam will test for both the numerical and discussion questions.

Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 2
General comments
This examiner’s report should be used in conjunction with the published
September/December 2024 sample exam which can be found on the ACCA Practice
Platform.
In this report, the examining team provide constructive guidance on how to answer the
questions whilst sharing their observations from the marking process, highlighting the
strengths and weaknesses of candidates who attempted these questions. Future
candidates can use this examiner’s report as part of their exam preparation, attempting
question practice on the ACCA Practice Platform
, reviewing the published answers
alongside this report.
The Financial Management (FM) exam is offered as a computer -based exam (CBE).
The model of delivery for the CBE exam means that candidates do not all receive the
same set of questions. In this report, the examining team offer detailed debriefs of
selected questions from each section of the exam.
• Section A objective test questions – we focus on four specific questions that
caused difficulty in the September/December 2024 sittings of the exam.
• Section B objective test case questions – here we look at one case from syllabus
area C in detail.
• Section C constructed response questions – here we provide commentary on two
questions, providing guidance on answering these questions and where exam
technique could be improved.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 3
Section A
In this section we will look at FOUR Section A questions which proved to be particularly
difficult for candidates.
Example one
Snow Co has in issue some 6% loan notes with a nominal value of $100 per loan note,
redeemable at a 13% premium to nominal value in four years' time. The required yield
to maturity is expected to be 8% per year. Snow Co currently pays tax at 20%.
What is the market value of each loan note (to two decimal places)?
options_zh:
["A. $93.37", "B. $98.95", "C. $100.00", "D. $102.93
What does this test?
 The application of appropriate valuation methods to redeemable debt
What is the correct answer?
 The correct answer is $102.93
Market value = $6 x 3.312 + $113 x 0.735 = $102.93
The most common errors involved ignoring the redemption premium and/or using a
post-tax coupon of $4.80 i.e. (1 – 0.2) x $6.
Example two
Which TWO of the following are NOT money market instruments?", "A. Bills of exchange", "B. Commercial paper", "C. Preference shares", "D. Banker’s acceptance", "E. Irredeemable loan note
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 4
What does this test?
 An understanding of the financial instruments that are available on the money
markets
What is the correct answer?
 The correct answer is C and E
This question tests candidates’ knowledge of the financial instruments that are
available on the m oney markets and more  specifically, the understanding that  the
money markets are geared towards short-term finance.  Irredeemable loan notes and
preference shares are long-term financial instruments that are not available on the
money markets.
Example three
Sunny Peaks Co has a current market capitalisation of $42m and has recently paid a
dividend of $7m.  The estimated annual growth rate for dividends is 2%.
What is the cost of equity for Sunny Peaks Co?
____________%
What does this test?
 The application of the dividend growth model
What is the correct answer?
 The correct answer is 19%
The formula for the dividend growth model is provided in the formula sheet.  The
formula needs to be rearranged in such a way that the cost of equity can be found, as
follows:
k = (d
0 x (1+g) / P) + g = (7m x 1.02 / 42m) + 0.02 = 19%
The most common errors involved not applying the growth rate to the current dividend
and/or incorrectly rearranging the dividend valuation formula, most often ignoring the
need to add the growth rate at the end of the formula i.e. d0 x (1+g) / P = 17%.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 5
Example four
Isterim Co is a large pharmaceutical company, listed on an internationally-recognised
stock exchange.  The board intends to raise finance of $150m in order to fund the
development of a new product  but needs advice on how best to raise this amount of
capital.
Which of the following intermediaries would be the best source of advice?", "A. Discount house", "B. Investment bank", "C. Unit trust", "D. Clearing bank
What does this test?
 An understanding of the role of financial intermediaries
What is the correct answer?
 The correct answer is B
An investment bank  will provide a range of banking services to its clients , including
advice on raising capital.
Discount houses trade in investments such as bills of exchange rather than provide
advice on corporate finance.  Unit trusts invest in securities to earn returns rather than
provide advice.  Clearing banks are concerned with providing daily banking services
rather than providing corporate finance advice.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 6
Section B
Section B tests candidates’ knowledge on a number of topics in more detail than
section A, with three case questions containing five two-mark objective test questions.
The following is an example case question on syllabus area G risk management.
Grift Co
Grift Co is based in a country whose currency is the dollar ($). The company has legal
expenses in a foreign country, Farnland, whose currency is the peso. These legal
expenses of 26m pesos must be paid within the next six months. Exchange rates
available to the company are as follows:
Pesos per $1
Spot exchange rate 8.0927 - 8.1250
Six-month forward exchange rate 7.8534 - 7.8889
The peso/dollar exchange rate has been volatile recently and the finance director of
Grift Co is wondering whether a lead payment might be better than hedging the
payment of legal expenses with a six-month forward exchange contract.
Grift Co has bank loans in the foreign country with a nominal value of 5m pesos that
are split equally between fixed rate and variable rate debt. The company has no assets
generating interest rate income. The finance director is concerned about the interes t
rate risk associated with these bank loans.
Question one
According to exchange rate theory, which of the following could explain the
current peso per $1 forward exchange rate of 7.8534 - 7.8889?", "A. The peso inflation rate is higher than the dollar inflation rate", "B. The supply of dollars is expected to decrease in the near future", "C. The peso interest rate is lower than the dollar interest rate", "D. The government of Farnland has been selling pesos from its official reserves
 The correct answer is C
If the peso interest rate is lower than the dollar interest rate, interest rate parity theory
indicates that the peso will strengthen relative to the dollar and therefore, the forward
rate will be lower than the spot rate.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 7
Question two
Which of the following statements about forecasting exchange rates is/are
correct?
(1) The international Fisher effect suggests that changes in exchange rates are
matched  by changes in real interest rates
(2) Under purchasing power parity, changes in domestic inflation rates are balanced
in the  longer term by changes in exchange rates", "A. 1 only", "B. 2 only", "C. Both 1 and 2", "D. Neither 1 nor 2
 The correct answer is B
The first statement is incorrect because the international Fisher effect predicts that the
spot exchange rate will change to offset nominal, rather than real, interest rate
differences between countries.
According to purchasing power parity  theory, the spot exchange rate will change to
offset the inflation rate differential between countries.  The second statement is
therefore correct.
Question three
If Grift Co decides to make a lead payment for the legal expenses rather than
using a six-month forward contract, what is the cost saving for the company (to
the nearest dollar)?
$ ____________
 The correct answer is $97,896
Grift Co needs to buy pesos .  Therefore, the  relevant spot rate is 8.0927 and the
relevant six-month forward rate is 7.78534
Dollar cost now = 26m/8.0927 = $3,212,772
Dollar cost in six months = 26m/7.8534 = $3,310,668
Cost saving = 3,310,668 - 3,212,772 = $97,896
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 8
The most common error involved using the rates for selling, rather than buying, pesos,
as follows:
Dollar cost now = 26m/8.1250 = $3,200,000
Dollar cost in six months = 26m/7.8889 = $3,295,770
Cost saving = 3,295,770 - 3,200,000 = $95,770
Question four
Which of the following statements about interest rate risk is/are correct?
(1) A negative gap exposure means that, for a given maturity date, interest -sensitive
liabilities are less than interest-sensitive assets
(2) The bank loans of Grift Co give rise to basis risk", "A. 1 only", "B. 2 only", "C. Both 1 and 2", "D. Neither 1 nor 2
 The correct answer is D
A negative gap exposure means that, for a given maturity date, interest -sensitive
liabilities are greater  than, not less than,  interest-sensitive assets. The statement
above refers to a positive gap rather than a negative gap exposure.
Basis risk would arise if there was a variable interest rate asset and a variable interest
rate liability where the variable rates are determined on different bases, which is not
the case with Grift Co.
Question five
Which of the following statements about interest rate derivatives is correct?", "A. A swap which exchanges fixed rate and variable rate interest in the same currency
should give benefit to both counterparties", "B. Interest rate futures contracts protect a borrower from an interest rate increase,
while allowing the benefit of a lower interest rate to be obtained", "C. An interest rate collar is created by selling a cap and selling a floor", "D. Over-the-counter interest rate options have standardised quarterly settlement
dates
 The correct answer is A
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 9
The outcome of an i nterest rate futures hedge will be the same whether there is an
increase or decrease in the interest rate.   Borrowers looking to protect themselves
from an interest rate increase will therefore be unable to benefit from an interest rate
decrease.
An interest rate collar is created by buying a cap and selling a floor ie not selling a cap.
Over-the-counter interest rate options are tailored to the needs of the parties and are
therefore not standardised.  Standardised quarterly settlement dates apply to
exchange-traded options rather than over-the-counter options.
Statement A is therefore the only correct statement.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 10
Section C
In this section we will look in detail at TWO constructed response questions from
different syllabus areas. The full questions and solutions have been published and are
available on the ACCA Practice Platform.
Galle Co
This question is from the Investment Appraisal  area of the syllabus  (section D) ,
specifically focussing on i nvestment appraisal techniques (D1), a llowing for inflation
and taxation in discounted cash flow (DCF) (D2) and adjusting for risk and uncertainty
in investment appraisal (D3).
Galle Co is a company which manufact ures car batteries and is evaluating the
introduction of a new battery called the G-Power Excel (GPE). Sales for the first year
are expected to be 10,000 units but there is uncertainty over sales volumes for the
remaining three years of the project’s life.
Requirement (a) – 12 marks
Part (a) is broken down into t wo requirements which require you to calculate the
expected net present value (NPV) of the GPE project and comment on its financial
acceptability.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 11
(a) For the GPE project
(i) Calculate the expected net present value; and
(10 marks)
(ii) Comment on its financial acceptability.
(2 marks)
The full solution can be found on the ACCA Practice Platform, and it shows the correct
approach to the calculations which should be studied.
The key to approaching the expected NPV calculation in the correct and most efficient
manner is to calculate the expected sales volume, based upon the predictions of the
sales volume for years 2 to 4. There is a 30% chance that the sales volume could drop
to 5,000 units per year for these years which, by deduction, means that there is a 70%
chance that sales volume per year will remain at the year 1 volume of 10,000 units.
Therefore:
Expected sales volume for years 2 to 4  = (0.3 * 5,000) + (0.7 * 10,000)
= 1,500 + 7,000
= 8,500 per year.
This is the sales volume which can be used for the volume-based cash flows in years
2 to 4 i.e. revenue and variable costs.
Incorrect approaches assum ed that 10,000 units would prevail for all four years (an
optimistic approach), 5,000 units would be the sales volume for years 2 to 4 (a
pessimistic approach), or took a simple average of the two volumes (7,500 units).
Once the sales volume figures had been ascertained,  they could then be used to
calculate revenue and variable costs  in each year . Care need ed to be taken to
correctly apply inflation to the price and variable cost per unit respectively. In particular,
the inflation % is stated “per year” which means that, for the sales price per unit, 4.5%
needs to be added cumulatively:
e.g. $70 * 1.045 = $73.15
$73.15 * 1.045 = $76.44 (alternatively $70 * 1.045
^2 )
and so on.
A similar approach needed to be taken with the unit variable cost of $45 and its specific
inflation rate of 3% per year. The fixed costs remained constant.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 12
As a result of the above, the correct taxable cash flow can be calculated and therefore
the tax-related cash flows can be addressed. “Galle Co pays corporation tax rate at a
rate of 20% and tax is paid at the end of the year to which it relates”. Therefore, the
tax payment should not be deferred by a year. Once again, this demonstrates the
importance of fully reading the question and not assuming when the tax -related cash
flows take place.
The suggested solution shows the alternative ways in which the benefits of tax -
allowable depreciation (TAD) can be calculated. The most important point  is not how
they’re calculated, but that the candidate shows the workings clearly so that they can
be followed and understood by the question marker. The showing of workings is vital
in addressing all computational requirements.
The most common errors arising for tax-related cash flows were:
- Not recognising that a balancing allowance arises at the end of this four -year
project;
- Calculating TAD, but using it in the computation as the TAD benefit i.e. not
applying the tax rate to the TAD;
- Incorrectly deferring tax-related cashflows (as above) when the question is clear
that they are paid at the end of the year to which they relate; and
- Not adding back TAD, after correctly deducting it to arrive at taxable profit (see
alternative calculation of after-tax cash flow in the suggested solution)
The working capital (WC) cash flows were not dependent upon sales and therefore
were more straightforward. As with the discussion regarding inflation above, it is
important to recognize that the WC required at the start of each year will rise
cumulatively by 3%:
e.g. $80,000 * 1.03 = $82,400, a WC injection of $2,400
$82,400 * 1.03 = $84,872*, a WC injection of $2,472
*(alternatively $80,000 * 1.03
^2 )
and so on.
This means that after the initial injection of $80,000 at the commencement of the
project, a further injection of $2,400 is required, then $2,472 and so on, until the WC
is recovered at the end of the project. It is important to remember that injections of WC
into the project are cash outflows, whilst the recovery of WC is a cash inflow.
Common errors regarding WC were mainly concerned with incorrect increments
and/or timing and/or direction of cash flow, with some responses simply ignoring WC
investment or assuming WC recovery was $80,000.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 13
At this point, having computed all of the above, the net cash flow for each year can be
computed, discounted at 13% and then the expected NPV can be calculated.
It is really important that calculations and supporting workings are set out clearly and
in an organised manner. Some candidates, unfortunately, set out the calculations in a
haphazard manner i.e. not in a columnar form, which makes candidate errors more
likely and makes it more difficult for a question marker to understand the candidate’s
logic. Where workings are clearly shown in the spreadsheet (either within a formula or
with clear labelling) marks can be awarded on the own figure rule (OFR) when minor
errors are made.
For part (a)(ii), for two marks, brief comments were required on the project’s financial
acceptability based upon the expected NPV calculated in (a)(i).
For one mark, a comment in line with that in the suggested solution would be sufficient
i.e. “the expected NPV is positive, therefore Galle Co can accept the project ”. Of
course, the mark is subject to the OFR in that if the candidate had calculated a
negative NPV in (a)(i), then a comment such as “expected NPV is negative, therefore
reject the project” would gain a mark.
For the second mark, reference needed to be made to the “expected” part of “expected
NPV”. Therefore, a reference to the expected volume of 8,500 units and it being based
upon the two possible sales volumes of 10,000 units or 5,000 units was required. Any
reasonable discussion using this information to state that the NPV would, with 70%
probability, be higher or, with 30% probability, be lower (or even negative) would have
been worth the second mark.
Utilising the spreadsheet response area
It is good practice for candidates to make full use of the spreadsheet functionality
available to them, and it is pleasing to see many candidates doing so.  It is essential,
however, that use of the spreadsheet software is practised as part of exam preparation
to ensure that where formulae are used, such as the NPV formula, that they are used
correctly.
The NPV formula needs particular attention as the formula assumes that the first cash
flow takes place at T1 and therefore the T0 cash flow needs to be accounted for
separately.
e.g.
Here is the expected NPV of the project net cash flows  via the NPV formula, with the
year 0 cash flow being the sum of the initial investment of $500,000 and initial working
capital of $80,000.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 14
Care must also be taken to express the discount rate as a % or as a decimal e.g. 0.13.
(Note that the expected NPV here differs slightly to that given in the suggested solution
due to the spreadsheet not rounding the discount factors to 3 decimal places.)
Using the NPV function for the whole of B3 to F3 will give the incorrect NPV.
A further point regarding spreadsheet use is that although it is possible to execute
some reasonably complex computations in one cell e.g. taxable cash flow or TAD
benefit (and some candidates are clearly very confident and highly proficient in
spreadsheet use), it may be better to set out the workings step by step as per the
suggested solution. This not only makes the presentation of a response clearer, but
makes it easier for a candidate to check their workings and the logic of their approach.
More generally, every effort should be made to avoid fundamental errors in
spreadsheet use e.g. double counting of cashflows by adding the whole column rather
selecting the relevant sub-totals.
Requirement (b) – 8 marks
(b) Discuss FOUR techniques for dealing with risk and uncertainty in an
investment appraisal.
(8 marks)
Part (b) tests section D3 of the syllabus, namely “Adjusting for risk and uncertainty in
investment appraisal” which includes specific areas on sensitivity analysis (D3b),
probability analysis (D3c) and other techniques of adjusting for risk and uncertainty in
investment appraisal including simulation, adjusted payback and risk -adjusted
discount rates (D3d).
The requirement allows discussion of any of the above mentioned techniques but it is
important to consider the exact wording of a requirement as well as the marks
available. Four techniques are required for eight  marks, hence two marks each,
suggesting that depth is required to each of the points made.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 15
For example, rather than a statement which states only “Probability analysis can be
used to find a single, weighted average sales volume for years 2 to 4”,  further
discussion could include some limitations of the technique, as shown in the suggested
solution.
Stronger responses to this requirement used the information provided in the scenario
to highlight aspects such as:
• T he expected sales volume (8,500 units) is dependent upon the accuracy of the
probabilities of each volume, as well as the volumes themselves , and these are
often not easy to predict, especially for a new battery, and
• Expected values only have reliable meaning for repeated events. The launch of
the GPE is highly unlikely to be a repetitive event.
These are examples of pieces of information which will enable a technique to be fully
discussed and hence two marks to be achieved for the technique.
A developed discussion was possible for the other techniques. For example, in
discussing sensitivity analysis, reference could have been made to the impact on
project NPV arising from movement in GPE’s sales volume for years 2 to 4 i.e. dealing
with uncertainty, or how sensitivity analysis does not give an indication of the likelihood
of such changes and is therefore not a technique for dealing with risk.
The suggested solution shows how discussion of simulation, adjusted payback and
risk-adjusted discount rate could also be developed in a way which could attract two
marks for each technique.
Many candidates made a good attempt at part (b) but too many provided only a brief
list of techniques with no discussion or typed “all they knew” about techniques such
as NPV, IRR and ROCE, which scored no marks. Time was also wasted on defining
risk and uncertainty which was not asked for.
The study of all previously published questions in the investment appraisal area will
be beneficial to candidates (see ACCA Practice Platform
.) In particular, the following
recent questions are relevant for the techniques tested in Galle Co:
Gini Co (March/June 2023)
Pulorough Co (September/December 2023)
Elaan Co (March/June 2024)
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 16
Guillermo Co
This question is from the Working Capital Management Syllabus C, a core area of the
syllabus in Financial Management, specifically focussing on the following sections:
• a pplying relevant accounting ratios, including: inventory turnover ratio, average
collection period and average payable period (C2b)
• discussing the cash operating cycle and the role of accounts payable, accounts
receivable and inventory holding period (C2a)
• a pplying relevant techniques in managing cash eg Miller-Orr model and discussing
the possible motives for holding cash (C2f).
Guillermo Co is a medium-sized company that manufactures a single product.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 17
Requirement (a) – 5 marks
(a) Calculate Guillermo Co’s cash operating cycle.
(5 marks)
This requirement should be relatively straightforward. A well -structured response
should include separate calculations for the cost of sales, credit sales, and credit
purchases. The complete solution is available on the ACCA Practice Platform, which
demonstrates the correct calculation approach and should be reviewed.
Overall, responses to this requirement were satisfactory, with most candidates
successfully calculating the cash operating cycle. However, the most common errors
included, for example:
• calculating raw material days based on cost of sales instead of raw material
purchases.
• f ailing to recognise that each inventory element requires an individual calculation.
• u sing total revenue rather than credit sales to determine receivables collection
days.
It is essential to recognise that since purchases are provided, utilising the cost of sales
as a proxy for the payables period is not necessary. Including all calculations will assist
candidates in identifying any errors. Additionally, each inventory element should have
its own distinct calculation and clarity in all workings is crucial.
Requirement (b) – 6 marks
(b)
Discuss THREE ways in which Guillermo Co could reduce its cash operating
cycle.
(6 marks)
This requirement needed to be applied to Guillermo Co's current situation. The clues
were in the scenario and the best answers clearly drew on the scenario.
It is important to consider the wording of a requirement and the marks available. In
part (b), three ways are required for six marks - hence two marks each, suggesting
that depth is required for the points made.  For example, rather than a statement such
as Guillermo Co's average receivables collection period of 68 days is much higher
than the standard terms of 30 days, the best answers considered the current problems
and discussed how to reduce the cash operating cycle.  This part of the question did
not specifically require further calculations.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 18
Strong responses to this requirement used the information provided in the scenario to
highlight aspects, for example:
• The current holding period for raw materials is 20 days, which, in light of the 3-day
production cycle in part (a), suggests that Guillermo Co's inventory of raw
materials may be considered excessive. If this is the case, it would be advisable
to reduce raw material holdings. Additionally, a brief discussion of just -in-time
purchasing options could facilitate a more thorough discussion of this issue,
potentially leading to the attainment of two marks.
Requirement (c) – 3 marks
(c)
Using the Miller -Orr model, calculate the target cash balance for Guillermo
Co.
(3 marks)
It should be noted that part (c) of this requirement required no knowledge of parts (a)
and (b). Many candidates were able to gain 2 or 3 marks from this part.
The Miller-Orr formula is provided in the formulae sheet.  The various criteria, along
with the values for daily standard deviation ($15,000), transaction cost ($55), the
annual interest rate (9%), and minimum cash or lower limit ($40,000), are provided in
the scenario under the sub-heading ‘cash management’.  These amounts should have
been used as follows:
• To calculate the ‘target cash balance’, it was necessary to utilise a structured
calculation set-up. First, calculate the ‘daily variance’ - remember variance is the
square of the daily standard deviation ($15,000
2). Second, consistency requires
calculating the daily interest rate (9% / 360 days). It is easier to calculate the above
two figures separately and then use them to calculate the ‘spread’ and the 'target
cash balance'’.
• In other questions, you may also be required to calculate the upper limit, which is
simply the sum of the lower limit and the spread.
•  Please note many candidates erroneously used 9% and/or $15,000 in their
calculation of the ‘spread’.
Utilising the spreadsheet response area
It is crucial to p ractice using spreadsheet software as part of your exam preparation.
This ensures that you can easily work with complex formulas, such as the 'spread', as
required in part (c). When your calculations are clearly presented in the spreadsheet—
whether through formulas or written explanations —marks can still be awarded for
minor errors.
Examiner’s report – FM September/December 2024 19
For long formulas like those needed for the 'spread' calculation, it may be beneficial to
compute different components in separate cells before combining them to determine
the final 'spread' value. This calculated 'spread' is then utilised to determine the 'target
cash balance', also called the 'return point’. It is crucial to practice using spreadsheet
software as part of your exam preparation. This ensures that you can work with
complex formulas, such as the 'spread' required in part (c), with ease. When your
calculations are clearly presented in the spreadsheet —whether through formulas or
written explanations— marks can still be awarded for minor mistakes. Based on the
information provided, an excel spreadsheet is shown below:
Requirement (d) – 6 marks
(c) Discuss the motives for a business to hold cash
(6 marks)
As per requirement (b), It is important to consider the wording of a requirement and
the marks available. In part (d), there are potentially two marks per motive, suggesting
that depth is required for each of the points made.  Many candidates gained 3 marks
from this part briefly explaining the three motives for holding cash. Candidates need
to provide more depth to obtain higher marks, ie providing an example or discussing
the downside for not holding cash."]

stem_zh: 1. Working capital management objectives (是什么？为什么冲突？）
2. Investment & financing policy 对比与联系
3. Credit policy 三个步骤
4. Managing foreign receivables
5. Indicators of overtrading（综合，需要体现逻辑）

1. Discuss  the objectives of WC management and why they might be in
conflict? 这题如果你考试抽到，不就是送分吗？
The main objective of working capital management is to get the balance of liquidity and
profitability.
后台能下载到的，即为最新版本（若有更新，课包资料会第一时间同步）
Profitability is allied to the financial objective of maximising shareholder wealth. If a
business operates with excessively low level of working capital, then this may lead to
trading problems, and lower profits.
A company must have sufficient cash to meet its liabilities, since otherwise it may fail.
Liquidity is needed in order to settle liabilities as they fall due and can be maintained by
ensuring that the amount of cash tied up in working capital is not excessive.
These two objectives may conflict. For example, if a decision is made to invest in higher
inventory or receivables in order to boost sales and profits, then this will tie up funds in
higher net working capital and this will reduce liquidity.

2. Discuss the difference between WC financing and investment policy
非常高频，选择题必考，这俩政策除了名字一样，其他都不一样。考官一直写文章说
大家区分不了，需要总结理解，再考概率依然很高。
第一个区别，定义不同：Working capital investment policy is concerned with the level of
investment compared with another. Working capital financing policy is concerned with the
relative proportions of short-term and long-term finance used by a company. 这俩定义必须要
能写出来。
第二个区别：Working capital financing policy uses an analysis of current assets into
permanent current assets and fluctuating current assets. Working capital investment policy
does not require this analysis. Permanent current assets represent the core level of investment in
current assets that supports a given level of business activity. Fluctuating current assets represent the
changes in the level of current assets that arise through, for example, the unpredictability of business
operations, such as the level of trade receivables increasing due to some customers paying late or the
level of inventory increasing due to demand being less than predicted.
第三个区别：Working capital financing policy relies on the matching principle, which is not
used by working capital investment policy. The matching principle holds that long-term assets
should be financed from a long-term source of finance. Non-current assets and permanent current
assets should therefore be financed from a long-term source, such as equity finance or bond finance,
后台能下载到的，即为最新版本（若有更新，课包资料会第一时间同步）
while fluctuating current assets should be financed from a short-term source, such as an overdraft or a
short-term bank loan.
唯一相同点---名字一样：Both working capital investment policy and working capital financing
policy use the terms conservative, moderate and aggressive.
但具体含义完全不同：In investment policy, the terms are used to indicate the comparative level of
investment in current assets on an inter-company basis. One company has a more aggressive approach
compared to another company if it has a lower level of investment in current assets, and vice versa for
a conservative approach to working capital investment policy.
In working capital financing policy, the terms are used to indicate the way in which fluctuating
current assets and permanent current assets are matched to short-term and long-term finance sources.
An aggressive financing policy means that fluctuating current assets and a portion of permanent
current assets are financed from a short-term finance source. A conservative financing policy means
that permanent current assets and a portion of fluctuating current assets are financed from a long-term
source. An aggressive financing policy will be more profitable than a conservative financing policy
because short-term finance is cheaper than long-term finance, as indicated for debt finance by the
normal yield curve (term structure of interest rates).
Overall, therefore, it can be said that while working capital investment policy and working
capital financing policy use similar terminology, the two policies are very different in terms of
their meaning and application. It is even possible, for example, for a company to have a
conservative working capital investment policy while following an aggressive working capital
financing policy.

3. Discuss techniques in managing trade receivables
不要只想得到 tools，credit policy 三个步骤本身就是管理应收的技巧，很多同学这个文
字题掌握很差，考前记得多熟悉。
Assessing creditworthiness
The company can seek to reduce its exposure to the risks of bad debt and late payment by assessing
the creditworthiness of new customers. In order to do this, the company needs to review information
from a range of sources. These sources include trade references, bank references, credit reference
后台能下载到的，即为最新版本（若有更新，课包资料会第一时间同步）
agencies and published accounts. To help it to review this information, Co might develop its own
credit scoring process. After assessing the creditworthiness of new customers, Co can decide on how
much credit to offer and on what terms.
Managing accounts receivable
Co needs to make sure that its credit customers follow the terms of trade agreed when credit was
granted by credit assessment. Key information here will be the number of overdue accounts and the
degree of lateness of amounts outstanding. An aged receivables analysis can provide this
information. Co also needs to make sure that its credit customers are aware of the outstanding
invoices on their accounts. The company will therefore remind them when payment is due and
regularly send out statements of account.
Collecting amounts owing
Ideally, credit customers will pay on time and there will be no need to chase late payers. If an account
becomes overdue, Co must make sure it is followed up quickly. Credit control staff must assess
whether payment is likely to be forthcoming and if not, a clear policy must be in place on further steps
to take. These further steps might include legal action and using the services of a debt collection
agency.
Offering early settlement discounts
Co can encourage its credit customers to settle outstanding amounts by offering an early settlement
discount. This will offer a reduction in the outstanding amount (the discount) in exchange for
settlement before the due date. Co must weigh the benefit of offering such an early settlement
discount against the benefit expected to arise from its use by credit customers. One possible benefit m
ight be a reduction in the amount of interest the company pays on its overdraft. Another possible
benefit might be matching or bettering the terms of trade of a competitor.
Using factoring and invoice discounting
Co might use a factor to help manage its accounts receivable, either on a recourse or non- recourse
basis. The factor could offer assistance in credit assessment, managing accounts receivable and
collecting amounts owing. For a fee, the factor could advance a percentage of the face value of
outstanding invoices. The service offered by the factor would be tailored to the needs of the company.
Invoice discounting is a service whereby a third party, usually a factor, pays a percentage of the face
value of a collection of high value invoices. When the invoices are settled, the outstanding balance is
paid to the company, less the invoice discounter’s fee.
后台能下载到的，即为最新版本（若有更新，课包资料会第一时间同步）

4. Discuss the difference between invoice discounting and factoring
帮助大家理解两者区别，从而攻克选择题，考大题几率极低，记关键词即可
Invoice discounting refers to the purchase of selected invoices by a financial company at a discount to
their face value. Invoice discounting can provide immediate cash （提供现金就是票据贴现唯一
的目的）to a company rather than waiting for the invoices to be settled. It tends to be used as an
occasional source of short-term finance, rather than a regular source of cash. Invoice discounting can
therefore aid in the management of trade receivables by accelerating cash inflow from trade
receivables when short-term cash flow problems arise.
Factoring refers to a commercial arrangement whereby a financial company takes over the
management of a company’s trade receivables（而保理指的是专业第三方接管应收账款的管
理，除了提供现金，还有记账/催款/开发票/提供坏账保险等服务）. This will include invoicing
customers, accounting for sales and collections of amounts owed. Factors will advance cash to a
company against the amounts outstanding. If the client requires, insurance against bad debts may also
be provided (non-recourse factoring).
Factoring can assist in the management of trade receivables through the expertise offered by the
factoring company. This may lead to a reduction in bad debts, a decrease in the level of trade
receivables, a decrease in the amount of managerial time devoted to chasing slow payers and taking
advantage of early settlement discounts from trade suppliers due to the availability of cash from trade
receivables.

5. Discuss the way of managing foreign trade receivables
Following methods may help an exporter manage its foreign receivables:

1. Reducing investment in foreign accounts receivable
A company can reduce its investment in foreign accounts receivable by asking for full or part
payment in advance of supplying goods. However, this may be resisted by consumers,
particularly if competitors do not ask for payment up front.

2. Export credit insurance
后台能下载到的，即为最新版本（若有更新，课包资料会第一时间同步）
Export credit insurance protects a business against the risk of non-payment by a foreign
customer. Export credit insurance usually insures the seller against commercial risks, such as
insolvency of the purchaser or slow payment, and also insures against certain political risks,
for example, war, which could result in non-payment. It can also protect against changes in
import or export regulations. Its disadvantages include the relatively high cost of premiums
and the fact that the insurance does not typically cover 100% of the value of the foreign sales.

3. Export factoring
An export factor provides the same functions in relation to foreign accounts receivable as a
factor covering domestic accounts receivable and therefore can help with the cash flow of a
business. However, export factoring can be costlier than export credit insurance and it may
not be available for all countries, particularly developing countries.

4. Bill of exchange
Bill of exchange is used primarily in international trade that binds one party to pay a fixed
sum of money to another party at a predetermined future date.
Bills of exchange is an IOU signed by the customer to the exporter. Bills of exchange can
either be discounted or negotiated by a company with its bank.
